observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a) Through the CGSO and CAO, reconcile the discrepancies between the
balances of the PPE sub-accounts recorded in the GL and those reflected
in the RPCPPE, and thereafter submit the reconciled report to the COA
Audit Team;
b) Through the City Architect’s Office and the City Engineer’s Office,
provide assistance to the CGSO, CAO and all other concerned offices in
locating and identifying missing items, particularly those related to
infrastructure projects, and thereafter, submit a detailed accomplishment
report on the findings;
c) Require all offices to comply with the minimum documentation and
reporting requirements prescribed under Section 57, Volume 2, of the
Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local
Government Units, and to include a summary of accounts and
corresponding amounts in their submissions for clarity and completeness;
d) Through CAO, reconcile the discrepancies between the balances of the
PPE accounts recorded in the GL and those reflected in the PPELCs, and
thereafter submit the reconciled report to the COA Audit Team, for
verification;
e) Conduct regular reconciliation of PPE accounts to ensure that all
acquisitions, transfers and dispositions of these assets are properly
documented, recorded and accurately reflected in the FS and other
pertinent accounting records;
f) Strictly observe and implement the provisions of Section 114 and 120 of the
MNGAS, as well as other applicable regulations governing the
management, utilization, and reporting of government property to prevent
future discrepancies in the maintenance of the PPE Account; and
g) Ensure through the CGSO and all other concerned offices of the City
Government, the strict compliance with Section 124, Volume 1, of the
MNGAS, as well as all other relevant provisions of the said manual
pertaining to PPE to guarantee the timely submission of the RPCPPE.

We recommended that the Management direct the City Accountant to:
a) Implement efficient planning and coordination with the City Treasurer to
process and pay only transactions with complete documentation, and to
avoid delays in submitting pertinent documents and/or reports, to ensure
proper recording in the books of accounts and accurate presentation of the
financial statements; and
b) Facilitate the preparation of the adjusting journal entries for valid
reconciling items such as the unrecorded disbursements and credit memos
in compliance with Section 3.3 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Require the City Accountant to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in
the accounting of supplies and materials and to ensure that proper account
is used to record transactions;
b) Instruct the City Accountant and General Services Officer to maintain and
reconcile the prescribed records such as SLCs and SCs to ensure proper
accounting and promote better control in the receipt and issuance of
supplies; and
c) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct the periodic physical count
of supplies or property and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCI
to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for
the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a) Maintain complete documentation of all the recorded obligations in the
Accounts Payable in compliance with the pertinent provisions of PD No.
1445, ensuring government obligations are valid and lawful, preventing
future legal issues with the creditors and the approving officers;
b) Maintain complete set of subsidiary ledgers and aging schedules for the
unpaid obligations of the City, so that the correctness and validity of the
payables could be verified and established in audit;
c) Revert and/or adjust the recorded liabilities which are not covered with
valid claims as well as the errors in recording; and
d) Correct the recording of the obligations of the City to the Accounts Payable
account using the contract cost of the projects, programs and activities
(PPAs), instead of the practice of using the estimated cost of PPAs.

We recommended that Management:
a) Conduct an immediate and thorough review, verification and
reconciliation of long outstanding payables, with particular emphasis on
transactions with lacking sufficient supporting documents and improperly
recorded in the Other Payables account, to ultimately identify and settle
only valid accounts. Then, submit a copy of the report to the COA Audit
Team for the purpose of review and reference;
b) Proactively coordinate with concerned agencies, offices, payees or other
relevant entities that the City has obligations involving trust fund accounts,
to clarify, validate, and facilitate the resolution of the outstanding
payables; and
c) Employ timely monitoring, reconciliation and settlement of the City’s
obligations, ensuring that all legitimate obligations are properly accounted
for and discharged/settled.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Require the City Treasurer to adopt measures which will ensure that
remittances are deposited promptly and intact in accordance with Section
32 of the NGAS for LGUs; and
b) Instruct the City Accountant to start a reconciliation process for the Cash
Local Treasury account and correct any errors or omissions that may have
caused the discrepancies in the account.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Ensure the proper granting, utilization, and liquidation of all cash
advances in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10,
1997; and
b) Direct the City Accountant to submit to COA a request for write-off of
dormant unliquidated cash advances aged 10 years and above, in
accordance with the guidelines and procedures in COA Circular No. 2023-
008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
a) Ensure the immediate remittance of the past due taxes and contributions
under the Inter-Agency Payables accounts particularly in the Due to BIR,
Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to GSIS;
b) Implement efficient planning and coordination with relevant offices to
avoid delays in submitting pertinent documents and ensure proper
recording in the books of accounts and accurate presentation of accounts
in the financial statements; and
c) Closely monitor the timely and complete remittance of all subsequent
monthly withholdings and contributions, in compliance with the
aforementioned laws, rules, and regulations.

We also recommended that the Management implement corrective action on
the inappropriate use of cash advance as a mode of payment to suppliers, in adherence
to sound internal control on handling cash under the imprest system by directly paying
suppliers with checks for the procurement of goods and services.

We recommended that Management:
a) Accelerate negotiations with potential investors to ensure the full
utilization of the Call Center Building and furnish the Audit Team with a
copy of the executed contract agreement to demonstrate compliance and
transparency in the leasing process;
b) Expedite the implementation of the AgRe-Eco Tourism Park project as an
alternative revenue-generating initiative to compensate for the
discontinued Amusement Park project;
c) Strengthen project planning, feasibility assessment and investor
commitments before undertaking future infrastructure projects financed
through loans to avoid similar financial burdens; and
d) Optimize loan utilization to effectively achieve its intended purpose.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Instruct the City Accountant to facilitate the immediate correction of the
computation of withholding taxes of concerned employees and remit the
corresponding taxes due to the BIR; and
b) Ensure strict compliance with tax laws and revenue regulations on tax dues
from compensation, bonuses and benefits of employees to avoid
unnecessary penalties that may be imposed by the BIR.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to check and monitor
suppliers of the CGI whose transactions will potentially exceed the VAT
threshold of ₱3,000,000.00 as well as initiate appropriate action to conform
and update their status in conformity with the requirements of the Tax
Code; and
b) Initiate appropriate action to ensure that all transactions with suppliers
are levied with the proper tax rates for remittance to BIR in accordance
with the Tax Code.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Direct the concerned management personnel and the City Accountant to
submit immediately the lacking documents to COA, properly transmitted,
filed and labelled according to project, for proper verification; and
b) Initiate appropriate action and instruct the City Accountant to enforce
consistently the submission of complete documents for the request of
contract review/technical evaluation of projects and for their subsequent
progress billings/payments in accordance with COA auditing rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Instruct the City Accountant to immediately submit the DVs and their
corresponding supporting documents totaling ₱118,747,757.73 to the
Office of the Auditor to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit; and
b) Initiate appropriate action to enforce the timely monthly submission of
LDF paid DVs and their supporting documents to COA from the various
offices involved in the LDF transactions to the Office of City Accountant.

We recommended that Management:
a) Submit immediately the lacking authorization for the City Mayor to enter
into contracts and the supplemental budgets approved by the SP for the
aforementioned implemented LDF PPAs in CY 2024, for verification;
b) Strictly adhere to budgeting rules and regulations in processing and
preparing the annual budget/appropriation of LDF;
c) Instruct the concerned personnel to ensure that all generic/lumpsum
appropriation and changes in the budget, if any are properly supported
with authorization and approved supplemental budget from the SP, in
compliance with Section 22 (c) and 321 of RA 7160, respectively; and
d) Ensure that the PPAs in the LDF budget/appropriation are
implementation-ready and promptly delivered to the public, in line with
the objectives of the 20% LDF.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Through the City HRMO, consider the creation of new permanent
positions for regular functions or the hiring of casual or contractual
personnel for projects and activities that are temporary in nature, subject
to the governing rules and regulations.
b) Consider engaging in institutional contract of services for functions that
are not permitted through individual contract of services but are necessary
to support the day-to-day operations of the City to ensure compliance with
COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2 s. 2020; and
c) Ensure that the duties and responsibilities in the contracts reflect the
actual functions to be performed by the COS personnel, and that the
project or job of the COS personnel is not part of the regular functions of
the City as set forth in Section 7.1 of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2 s.
2020.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the submission of all contracts,
including Contracts of Service and Project-based Contracts, to COA within five (5)
working days from its perfection to facilitate auditorial review in compliance with
COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Management strictly adhere to the guidelines,
rules, and regulations on the reporting of the sources and utilization of the LDRRMF,
and ensure that properly accomplished monthly utilization reports, RAAOs, and
SAOBs are submitted for audit. Moreover, proper validation and monitoring of the
implementation of PPAs should be conducted regularly to ensure the attainment of
disaster risk reduction and management outcomes for which the fund allocation is
intended.

We recommended that Management:
a) Discontinue the use of cash advance for the procurement of goods, supplies,
and materials for the implementation of PPAs and instead resort to
applicable mode of procurement provided and prescribed by the
government procurement law;
b) Implement and strengthen effective and efficient monitoring techniques in
the timing of implementing the PPAs of the City Government to give ample
time to process its implementation in accordance with various applicable
rules and procedures set by regulatory bodies of the government; and
c) Ensure that cash advance for travel expenses for several payees is properly
supported with duly paid and acknowledged/signed payroll, for
transparency and accountability purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to immediately submit
the DVs and their corresponding supporting documents totaling
₱25,564,227.00 to COA to avoid suspensions and/or disallowances in audit,
and
b) Initiate appropriate action to enforce the timely monthly submission of
LDRRMF paid DVs and their supporting documents to COA.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) Require the concerned local officials to strictly comply with the utilization
policies for the LDRRMF as prescribed by pertinent laws, rules, and
regulations to avoid unrelated/irregular expenditures that may result in the
issuance of a Notice of Disallowance; and
b) Instruct the City Accountant to immediately submit the necessary
supporting documents to substantiate the payments made for the identified
transactions charged to the LDRRMF, to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that Management direct the GFPS to:
a) Establish and/or strengthen their monitoring and evaluation system for
GAD-related expenses through planning and coordination with the City
Accountant to ensure a proper assessment of the effectiveness and
efficiency of the City’s gender mainstreaming efforts; and
b) Submit a copy of the GAD AR and other required supplementary reports
to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Direct the GFPS to strictly adhere to the planning and budgeting
requirements for the formulation of annual GPB as prescribed under
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
b) Instruct the GAD Focal person to establish and/or strengthen the
monitoring and evaluation system for the effective and efficient
implementation of each GAD PPAs to ensure the timely and accurate
submission of GAD AR and other GAD-related reports;
c) Require the GAD Focal person in respective offices to strictly comply with
the utilization policies for the GAD fund as prescribed by pertinent laws,
rules, and regulations to avoid unrelated/irregular expenditures that may
result in the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance (ND);
d) Discontinue the practice of splitting and using cash advances for the
procurement of goods, supplies, and materials for the implementation of
GAD PPAs, which may result to irregular transactions and issuance of
ND, if warranted. Instead, resort to the applicable modes of procurement
provided and prescribed by the government procurement law; and
e) Instruct the City Accountant to submit DVs and their supporting
documents within the set reglementary period and immediately submit
the unsubmitted DVs and necessary supporting documents for GAD-
related transactions for CY 2024 to the Audit Team for verification.

We recommended that the Management:
a) Strictly adhere to the procurement procedures bounded by laws, rules and
regulations of RA No. 9184 in undertaking procurement of goods and
services;
b) Ensure that all schedules of activities of the City are properly monitored
to give lead time to procurement process so that it is timely undertaken and
avoids hasty and problematic transactions;
c) Submit sufficient evidence and/or required supporting documents to
establish the validity and legality of the disbursement made, to avoid
suspension and/or disallowance in audit; and
d) Employ appropriate action to implement strict compliance with
appropriate supporting documents and checking of its completeness before
signing and approving disbursements.

We recommended that Management direct the CEO, Architect’s Office,
CPDO or any other responsible Local Department Heads to consistently prepare and
submit duly Approved List of all on-going government projects/programs/activities
(PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year to the Supervising
Auditor and Audit Team Leader at the beginning of the year pursuant to Item No.
2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 for verification, updated if
need arises.
We also recommended strict compliance to the provisions of Section 3 of the
same COA Circular on posting requirements thru a standard Project Signboard as
designed and its notification to COA, as well as the submission of the duly signed
Monthly Monitoring Report and/or a Statement of Work Accomplishments (SWA)
based on the interval of verification (%) percentage provided for in the Table.

We recommended that Management:
a) Conduct a more detailed feasibility study to ensure project viability before
commencement, including detailed engineering investigation, surveys and
designs, along with the corresponding ROW acquisition, to anticipate
possible deviations and ensure precise cost estimation;
b) Require a thorough validation of construction plans before project
implementation to minimize the necessity for variation orders;
c) Implement stricter controls to ensure contractors strictly adhere to the
agreed-upon plans and specifications and enforce stricter material testing
and quality control procedures at various stages of the project to prevent
the use of inferior materials;
d) Require the contractor to replace substandard materials at their own cost
and impose penalties for non-compliance with contract specifications, for
all on-going and future contracts/projects; and
e) Blacklist or penalize contractors who repeatedly fail to adhere to contract
specifications to deter further violations.

a) provide proper and complete documentation for unpaid obligations totaling
₱44,912,341.45, otherwise they shall be deemed invalid and therefore must not
be paid in subsequent year unless there is compliance with some documentary
requirements; and
b) stop the practice of obligating or certifying unpaid obligations to Accounts
Payable or Other Payables unless they are based on valid claims, supported
with evidence, and related projects/programs have been completed or
goods/services delivered, to avoid misstatements in the balance of said
account at any given time.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer to release/remit
barangay RPT shares automatically to component barangays within five days after the
end of each quarter, without need of further demand and not subject to any lien or
holdback for whatever purpose.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer to cause the
transfer of the amount of ₱14,762,320.57 representing the unexpended balances of
LDRRMF for 2018 and immediately preceding years to the special trust fund account.

We also recommended that the City Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher
upon transfer of the fund to correct the understatement by the same amount of both the
Subsidy to Other Funds and Trust Liability-DRRMF accounts.

We further recommended that Management direct the Acting City Accountant to
provide adequate disclosure of the unutilized QRF and DRRMF-MOOE in the Notes to
Financial Statements at year-end.

We recommended that Management prepare a well-designed guideline that is
clear and transparent and can be implemented uniformly so that deserving of the
scholarship/study grant can avail easily.

We also recommended that a Screening Committee be instituted by the City so
that a thorough screening of the applicants could be made to ensure that student
beneficiaries are all qualified and deserving of the grant.

We further recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to
facilitate disbursement for scholarship grants/expenses thru direct payment to the school
where the grantee was enrolled and stop the payment thru cash advance.

We recommended that the City Mayor cause for the immediate submission of the
300 DVs totaling ₱58,633,781.06 to the Office of the Audit Team Leader, otherwise, the
entire unsubmitted accounts shall be suspended in audit.

We also recommended that the City Mayor initiate the necessary administrative
action to enforce the timely and complete submission of the monthly reports and accounts
from the Office of the City Treasurer to the Office of City Accountant and eventually to
the Audit Team in compliance with Section 7 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that Management direct the City officials concerned to
implement the identified GAD projects to achieve the objectives of the government
regarding the institutionalization of Gender and Development in the communities.

We also recommended that the GAD council should strictly conform to the
guidelines for the preparation of the Annual Gender and Development (GAD) Plan and
Budget and Accomplishment Report. The same should be geared towards the
development of programs, projects and activities that promote gender responsive
governance.

We recommended that Management, in the preparation of the Annual Investment
Plan, include only those programs/projects that will partake the nature of investment or
capital expenditures prescribed under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2017-1
to ensure that the 20% EDF is optimally utilized.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer to intensify the tax
campaign by sending treasury personnel to barangays especially during barangay
assemblies for tax information drive in order to invite more taxpayers to pay taxes on
time.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Acting City Accountant to:
a) prepare the necessary accounting entries to adjust the overstatement in the
RPT and SET receivables as well as in the Deferred RPT and SET accounts;
and
b) ensure that the amount collected as payment for RPT and SET regardless of
what year it pertains shall be credited to RPT and SET Receivable and be
debited to Deferred RPT/SET accounts.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer, the
Cashier, the OIC-City Accountant, and all treasury and accounting
personnel concerned to (a) trace the errors or omissions that caused the
discrepancies in the GL/SL and Cashbook balances of the CLT and CIB-
LCCA accounts from January to December, 2016; (b) foot, rule, and close
their respective books daily and monthly; and (c) reconcile their
reciprocal records to bring them into agreement, at least, at the close of
every quarter.

We recommended that Management demand immediate and full
liquidation of all cash advances for special events, programs and other
time-bound activities that were already deemed, and were expected to be,
accomplished or completed to this day to avoid suspensions and/or

disallowance in audit and to avoid being reported to proper agencies for
the filing of appropriate charges under COA Memorandum Nos. 2004-
014 and 2005-074 dated February 24, 2004 and September 15, 2015,
respectively.

We recommended that Management (a) demand immediate and
full liquidation of all cash advances for regular travels, seminars, and
other time-bound activities that were already deemed, and are expected
to be, accomplished or completed to this day to avoid suspensions and/or

disallowance in audit and to aveid being reported to proper agencies for
the filing of appropriate charges under COA Memorandum Nos. 2004-
014 and 2005-074 dated February 24, 2004 and September 15, 2015,
respectively.

We also recommended that Management (b) stop granting
unauthorized cash advances to employees not designated as disbursing
officers and non-employees of the City and (c) require immediate
liquidation of all outstanding cash advances given to these people to avoid
suspensions or disallowances in andit.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC-City
Accountant to (1) trace, analyze, and correct possible errors in recording
and (2) secure relevant records of loan releases and repayments from
PNB and DBP to facilitate immediate and accurate reconciliation of
outstanding loan balances in the ensuing periods.

We recommended that Management (a) stop giving additional
cash advances to SDOs who have not yet settled their previous cash
advances; (b) state the legally specific purpose for which any cash
advance is to be given; and (c) require proper, complete, and relevant
documentation of all expenses charged to and paid from the cash
advances of SDOs to avoid suspemsions and/or disallowances in audit.

We also recommended that Management (d) revoke the

designations and (e) stop the grant of special cash advances to
Department Heads-cum-SDOs who fail to make full, prompt, and
proper

liquidation of their cash advances and who perform the incompatible
functions of authorization and disbursements of funds under their
respective offices, to avoid possible abuse or misuse of scarce government
resources.

We recommended that Management stop, or minimize and
restrict, the grant of immoderate (a) cash advances and (b)
reimbursements to SDOs and other City officials who make payments for
expenses that could and should have been rightfully incurred and paid
following the basic rules on public bidding, withholding of taxes, and
direct payment to suppliers. We also recommended that, aside from
holding them responsible or liable for audit suspensions and/or
disallowances, appropriate administrative sanctions should be imposed
upon those who continuously fail to comply with the requisite
documentation of claims, or otherwise make false or bloated invoices in
their liquidation and/or reimbursement vouchers.

We recommended that Management: (a) exercise restraint and
prudence in the utilization of scarce government resources for meals,
snacks, and other grocery and food items consumed or served during
LGU-sponsored activities, events, and/or meetings; (b) diligently and
judiciously plan all official activities that demand food and representation
expenses, taking into account the necessity and reasonableness of food
provision, the reasonable number of participants, and the timing and
frequency of the meetings/activities undertaken; and (c) require that
procurement of meals, snacks, and other food items be supported with
proper and complete documentation to avoid suspemsions or

disallowances in audit.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of spending
public funds on meals and snacks served durisg ordinary management
and legislative meetings and on consumable grocery and food packs
deemed for consumption, entertainment, or reception of staff and guests
in some Offices of the City. Public funds are property of the people,
hence they must be used prudently at all times with a view to prevent
dissipation and waste. Failure to comply with the ‘public purpose
requirement’ of the law shall be a ground for disallowance of the claims
in audit.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of giving: (a)
honoraria to LSB members; and (b) additional compensation, bonus,
or allowances to nationally-hired teaching and non-teaching personnel of
public elementary and secondary schools in the City who are not
handling extension classes, to avoid disallowances in audit.

We recommended that Management:

(a) institutionalize the grant of AICS or financial assistance to the needy
through the CSWDO, which is the specific Office mandated to
conduct prior study, evaluation, and recommendation of qualified
recipients of the program;

(b) discontinue the practice of allowing reimbursements for AICS or
financial assistance to City officials who are not authorized to handle
nor disburse funds for the purpose. Disbursements of this kind
should be made either through (1) cash advance of a designated
special disbursing officer for AICS or (2) direct payment in check to
clients after careful study, evaluation, and recommendation by the
CSWD Officer;

() extend AICS only once to eligible and needy clients in amounts not
exceeding $2,000.00 (for crisis situations other than death) and
$5,000.00 (for burial). If, by chance, repetitive AICS cannot be
avoided to clients who chronically seek assistance, require the social
worker to make a thorough assessment of the case to determine the
root cause of the problem and to decide what other support services
can best meet the needs of those clients;

(d) prioritize and make the grant of AICS exclusive only to bona fide and
needy constituents of the City, and stop granting the same to
constituents of other LGUs or political subdivisions which are beyond
the jurisdiction of the City Government; and

(e) stop granting financial assistance to private persons or groups that do
not meet the public purpose requirement of the law.

We also recommended that (f) financial assistance given to
barangay officials and DepEd personnel should be given directly to the
Barangays and agency concerned and fully receipted by their respective
treasurers or cashiers to emsure proper accountability and accounting
thereof. Thereafter, they should also be liquidated with the City
Government as soon as the purposes for which they were given have been
served.

We recommended that Management (1) limit the funeral/burial
assistance per client to £5,000.00 only, as directed under DSWD AO No.
5, 5. 2008; and (2) refund excessive funeral/burial assistance in CY 2016
totaling $265,000.00 to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management (a) direct the City Treasurer
to release/remit all prior years’ barangay RPT shares. We also
recommended that (b) RPT shares for CY 2017 and onwards be released
automatically to component barangays within five days after the end of
each quarter, without need of further demand and not subject to any lien
or holdback for whatever purpose. Lastly, we recommended that
Management (c) demand full and immediate refund of excess RPT shares
from barangays concerned to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Manapement direct the BAC and the Accounting Office to submit separate sets or copies of contract
documents, final payment vouchers, and their supporting documents to
the COA Auditor to facilitate timely technical review and inspection of
completed infrastructure projects. Inability to submit these documents
within the time prescribed by existing regulations shall be a cause for
suspension of the tramsactions in audit, without prejudice to the
administrative liability of the erring public officers or employees who are
responsible for such failure.

We recommended that Management direct all City officials
concerned, especially the CDRRM Officer, to plan, allocate, and
implement PPAs under the 5% LDRRMTF solely and strictly for disaster
prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation, and
recovery purposes, to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer and
the OIC-City Accountant to: (a) transfer or credit the amount of P13.931
million, representing the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF from
CYs 2012 to 2016, to a special trust liability account in the Trust Fund;
(b) provide adequate disclosure of the unutilized QRF and DRRMF-
MOOE at year-end; (c) maintain separate subsidiary ledgers per source
and year to keep track of the balances of these special trust funds within

the 5-year validity period fixed by law; and (d) revert back to the General
Fund the unexpended QRF balance in CY 2011 amounting to
P725,466.50.

We recommended that Management (a) direct the Accounting
Office to submit the above-mentioned 147 pieces of paid DVs and their
supporting documents to the COA Auditor to avoid suspensions in audit.
We also recommended that (b) the City Treasurer and the OIC-City
Accountant take hands-on and diligent review and supervision over

delegated works to staff to improve and expedite submission of accounts
and their supporting documents in the ensuing periods.

We recommended that Management establish clear-cut policies
and guidelines for the Scholarship Program to out-of-school youth. The
qualifications and documentary requirements should be well defined to
ensure that only qualified and deserving applicants are granted with
scholarship on vocatioral courses offered by the City to the OSY.

We recommended that Management (1) require immediate refund
of undeducted withholding taxes from payments out of cash advances and
reimbursements in CY 2016; and (2) remit all refunds so received to the
BIR to avoid further penalties or surcharges. Henceforth, we also
recommended that Management direct the Accounting Office to (3)
deduct appropriate withholding taxes from all forms of future money
payments, whether made on cash advance or reimbursement basis, to
comply with existing tax laws and regulations.

‘We recommended that Management remove the prohibited mark
or signage on a public building to comply with the requirement of Section
13 (d) of RA 7160.

‘We recommended thiit Management uphold and adhere strictly to
the exclusive enumeration of official signatories on checks under Section
345 of RA No. 7160, to avoid undue exercise of executive powers by the
City Mayor.

‘We recommended that Management require the SDOs to create,
maintain, and update their individual cashbooks for special purpose cash
advances, to facilitate awdit and examination of their accounts at least
once in every year as directed by this Commission.

‘We recommended that Management keep a modest yet meaningful
celebration of its annual cultural events by regulating and limiting the
festival expenses only to those that are official, legal, relevant, and
necessary to the occasion/s.

‘We also recommended that Management adhere to the basic rules
on government procurement and payment (particularly the rules on
public bidding, direct payment to suppliers/creditors, withholding of
taxes, and proper documentation of festival expenmses), to avoid
suspensions and/or disallowances in audit.
